title: GMP hash manufacturing: facility flow and quality control
sub: A from-scratch guide to designing a clean, compliant hash and extract factory — what GMP means, how rooms and people and product flow, and how a batch earns its way to market.

sections.title: What This Is: GMP and the Hash Lab From Zero
sections.summary: GMP stands for Good Manufacturing Practice — a documented, audited system that proves a product is what its label says and that nothing harmful rode along. A GMP hash lab turns cannabis biomass into purified resin concentrates (bubble hash, rosin, live resin, distillate) under contamination control, because the same physics that concentrate cannabinoids also concentrate contaminants. The whole design answers one question at every step: can we prove this material is safe and correctly labelled? If yes it moves forward; if not, it holds. This paper is a reference architecture, not legal advice — limits vary by jurisdiction.
points: GMP = a written, validated, inspectable system; 'if it isn't written down, it didn't happen'
Concentration is the core risk: extraction multiplies both the good and the bad roughly 5–10x
Five cleanliness grades (CNC through Grade A) and seven mandatory release-test families anchor the design
Zero open critical deviations are permitted at the point of release

figures.name: Standards-to-facility map
figures.desc: The governing standards (EU-GMP Annex 1, cGMP 21 CFR 210/211, ICH Q7/Q9/Q10, ISO 14644-1, GACP, NFPA 30/C1D1) and which facility area each one governs

figures.name: The four headline numbers
figures.desc: Visual of the anchor stats: 5 cleanliness grades, 7 release test families, 100% genealogy coverage, 0 critical deviations at release

claims_needing_cite: Extraction concentrates cannabinoids and contaminants roughly 5–10x
EU-GMP Annex 1 governs cleanroom classification and contamination control strategy
cGMP 21 CFR 210/211 governs production controls, records, and QC unit release authority

sections.title: Key Terms: The Words You Need First
sections.summary: Before any diagram makes sense, a handful of terms do the heavy lifting. A cleanroom grade (CNC, D, C, B, A) describes how clean the air is, mapped to ISO 14644 particle classes (e.g. Grade C = ISO 7, Grade A = ISO 5). A batch is one defined production run with a single lot ID; a deviation is any departure from the approved process; CAPA is the corrective-and-preventive-action loop that closes it. QA (Quality Assurance), not production, owns the final release decision.
points: Cleanroom grade vs ISO class: Grade D = ISO 8, Grade C = ISO 7, Grade A = ISO 5 at the fill point
Quarantine / Released / Rejected — the three exclusive status states every material sits in
CCP (Critical Control Point): a step where a measured limit prevents a hazard (HACCP language)
ALCOA+: records must be Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate (plus Complete, Consistent, Enduring, Available)
Residual solvent, water activity (Aw), CoA (Certificate of Analysis), genealogy, C1D1 (flammable-rated room)

figures.name: Grade-to-ISO quick reference
figures.desc: Each cleanroom grade beside its ISO 14644 class, air changes per hour, and the activity that happens there

claims_needing_cite: ISO 14644-1 defines cleanroom particle-count classifications including ISO 5, ISO 7, and ISO 8
HACCP critical-control-point methodology is the basis for in-process limits in food and pharma manufacturing

sections.title: Zoning and Cleanroom Grades: Nested Shells
sections.summary: The building is laid out as nested cleanliness shells: dirty operations (intake, milling, waste) sit at the perimeter and the cleanest operations (collection, filling, packaging of open product) sit at the core. Product moves inward toward purity; people and waste move outward toward the dirty edge, and the two never cross uncontrolled. Each shell boundary is an airlock plus a one-grade step. Most recreational and medical hash operations run a D-to-C envelope and treat the fill point as Grade C with local protection — Grade A/B is only needed for sterile or pharma-grade dose forms.
points: Goods-in/quarantine is CNC (4–6 air changes/hr); milling and dispensing are Grade D / ISO 8 (10–20 ACH)
Wash/extraction and freeze-dry/press are Grade C / ISO 7 (20–40 ACH)
Solvent recovery is a C1D1-rated Grade D room with LEL purge
Open-product fill point reaches Grade A / ISO 5 with unidirectional airflow only for pharma-grade fill

figures.name: Nested-shell facility map
figures.desc: Concentric zoning from CNC outer shell through Grade D, C, B to a Grade A core, with green arrows for inward product flow and blue dashed arrows for outward personnel and waste flow

figures.name: Room classification reference
figures.desc: Each room with its grade, ISO class, air changes per hour, and key activity

claims_needing_cite: Grade C cleanrooms correspond to ISO 7 and Grade A to ISO 5 under EU-GMP Annex 1
Recommended air-change rates of 20–40 ACH for ISO 7 cleanrooms

sections.title: Pressure Cascade, HVAC, and Gowning
sections.summary: Air is the primary contamination vector, so a positive-pressure cascade keeps clean rooms blowing air outward into dirtier rooms — opening a door can never suck contamination toward product. Pressures step up from 0 Pa (CNC) to +15 Pa (D), +30 Pa (C), +45 Pa (B), +60 Pa (A); solvent rooms invert this to about -15 Pa to contain flammable vapour and pull it to the LEL exhaust. People are the largest particle and microbial source, so entry is a one-way airlock sequence that escalates gowning as the grade rises, with a physically separate de-gown exit corridor. Anyone with open wounds, respiratory illness, or GI symptoms is excluded at the health check and it is recorded.
points: Pressure ladder: CNC 0 Pa, D +15, C +30, B +45, A +60; solvent C1D1 room runs negative at about -15 Pa
Filtration scales with grade: F9 in prep, H13 HEPA in wash/dry, H14 HEPA at fill
Gowning escalates: lab coat + single glove (D) to coverall + double glove (C) to sterile one-piece + double glove (B/A)
Airlocks are interlocked so both doors never open together; entry and egress never share a door

figures.name: Pressure cascade ladder
figures.desc: Stepped boxes showing rising positive pressure from CNC to Grade A with airflow arrows always running clean-to-dirty, plus the negative solvent room

figures.name: Gowning airlock cascade
figures.desc: Left-to-right one-way gowning steps from CNC entry through grade airlocks to cleanroom work, with a separate dashed de-gown exit

figures.name: HVAC control matrix
figures.desc: Temperature, RH, filtration, delta-P, ACH, and recirculation targets per room grade

claims_needing_cite: EU-GMP Annex 1 recommends a pressure differential of roughly 10–15 Pa between adjacent classified zones
H14 HEPA filters achieve at least 99.995% retention at MPPS per EN 1822
Personnel are the dominant source of particulate and microbial contamination in cleanrooms

sections.title: How the Product Is Actually Made
sections.summary: Two routes leave the weigh-in: solventless and solvent extraction. Solventless mechanically separates trichome heads with ice-water agitation, then sieves (220 to 25 micron), freeze-dries, and presses to rosin — its four critical control points are wash temp (<=4 C), water quality (RO, <10 CFU/mL), water activity (Aw <=0.55), and press temp (<=90 C). Solvent extraction is a closed loop (butane, ethanol, or CO2) where the dominant hazards shift to flammability and residual solvent, gated hard by a GC-MS/headspace test before release. For solventless, water is treated as an ingredient with its own RO/DI loop, UV + 0.2 micron polish, and sampling at three points.
points: Solventless CCPs: wash <=4 C, RO water <10 CFU/mL, final Aw <=0.55, press <=90 C
Solvent residual limits: n-butane/propane <=2000–5000 ppm (jurisdiction-dependent), ethanol <=5000 ppm (Class 3)
Solvent rooms need C1D1 interlocks: LEL gas detection + auto-purge, explosion-proof electrics, two-person rule, ASME-rated vessels
Water loop: mains to carbon/sediment pre-filter to RO/DI to UV+0.2 micron to sanitized ice hopper to point of use

figures.name: Solventless swimlane with CCPs
figures.desc: Input, process, and CCP/QC swimlanes for fresh-frozen biomass through agitate, sieve, collect, freeze-dry, and press with the four critical control points called out

figures.name: Closed-loop solvent extraction
figures.desc: Charge column to solvent pass to filter/winterize to recovery to vacuum purge, with the residual-solvent QC gate and parallel C1D1 safety interlock overlay

figures.name: Treated water and ice loop
figures.desc: Linear water-treatment train with the three sampling points and out-of-spec quarantine logic

claims_needing_cite: Residual butane/propane action limits of 2000–5000 ppm vary by US state and other jurisdictions
Ethanol is an ICH Q3C Class 3 solvent with a typical limit around 5000 ppm
C1D1 (NFPA classified) rooms are required for hydrocarbon extraction with LEL detection and explosion relief
Water activity at or below 0.55–0.65 suppresses microbial and fungal growth in concentrates

sections.title: Step-by-Step: Testing, Sampling, and Batch Release
sections.summary: Testing happens at three tiers — incoming biomass, in-process, and release — across four sampling stations along the value stream, with retained reference samples kept to expiry + 1 year. The finished-goods release panel is the legal gate to market and covers seven families: potency (HPLC-DAD), residual solvents (headspace GC-MS), pesticides (LC-MS/MS, GC-MS/MS), microbials (TYMC, TAMC, E. coli, Salmonella, Aspergillus), heavy metals (ICP-MS for Pb/Cd/As/Hg), mycotoxins (LC-MS/MS), and water activity/moisture. QA — not production — then runs three sequential gates: batch record complete and signed, all results within spec, all deviations closed; any 'no' diverts to remediation and only a clean pass on all three reaches release with a QP/QA signature and issued CoA.
points: Always test the finished concentrate — a pesticide level that passes on flower can fail once concentrated
Release panel = 7 mandatory families; pathogens (Salmonella, E. coli) must be absent
Release is a three-gate decision tree: records signed, results in spec, deviations closed
Sampling is itself an SOP: defined sample size, sterile tools, retention samples kept to expiry + 1 year

figures.name: Four sampling stations
figures.desc: Timeline from incoming biomass (S1) through in-process (S2) and bulk concentrate (S3) to finished goods (S4) with what each station tests

figures.name: Finished-goods release panel
figures.desc: Seven test families with analytes, method, typical action limit, and rationale

figures.name: Batch release decision tree
figures.desc: Three sequential yes/no gates (records, results, deviations) routing to QA release or to return/OOS/hold lanes

claims_needing_cite: Cannabis concentrate release panels typically require potency, residual solvents, pesticides, microbials, heavy metals, mycotoxins, and water activity testing
ICP-MS is the standard method for Pb, Cd, As, and Hg quantification in cannabis products
Aflatoxins B1/B2/G1/G2 and Ochratoxin A are carcinogenic mycotoxins regulated at ppb levels

sections.title: When It Goes Wrong: Deviations, CAPA, and Common Traps
sections.summary: When reality departs from the approved process, the deviation system captures it through a five-stage CAPA loop: detect/log, contain (quarantine and scope), investigate root cause (5-why or fishbone), correct the batch, then prevent and verify with an SOP/training update and an effectiveness check that feeds back into the process. Severity triage sets the urgency: critical (patient-safety or recall risk) goes to the QA director with <24h containment, major to QA on a defined timeline, minor to a supervisor to be trended. The classic traps are extrapolating safety from the biomass CoA instead of testing the concentrate, releasing on a borderline residual-solvent result, and letting physical status disagree with system status.
points: CAPA loop is five stages and is not closed until the effectiveness check passes
Severity drives sign-off level and clock: critical = QA director + <24h containment
Never release on a borderline residual solvent result — re-test, re-purge, or reject, and document the disposition
Physical and system status must always agree; quarantine stock stays behind a locked cage or controlled rack

figures.name: Five-stage CAPA loop
figures.desc: Detect, contain, investigate, correct, prevent+verify with a dashed feedback arrow closing the loop, plus a severity-triage band

figures.name: Waste and reject disposition
figures.desc: Off-spec product and spent biomass into a locked quarantine cage, rendered unusable, then licensed disposal with witnessed manifest; solvent waste as a separate hazmat stream

claims_needing_cite: ICH Q10 establishes the pharmaceutical quality system including CAPA and change control
Root-cause tools such as 5-why and Ishikawa (fishbone) are standard deviation-investigation methods
Cannabis waste must typically be rendered unusable and disposed via a licensed hauler with a witnessed manifest

sections.title: Realistic Expectations: Cost, Cadence, and What 'Compliant' Really Means
sections.summary: Compliance is a continuous program measured by data, not a one-time build: management review tracks KPIs like right-first-time release rate (target >=98%), deviation rate per batch (<5%), median CAPA closure (<=30 days), EM results within action limits (>=95%), and mock-recall retrieval (<24h). Equipment must be qualified through the V-model (DQ/IQ/OQ/PQ) before it makes releasable product, followed by process validation of three conformance batches, and cleaning is validated against measurable MACO carryover limits — not 'looks clean.' Scale the gowning, monitoring, and grade to what you actually run: a D-to-C envelope is normal for most hash operations, and over-building to Grade A/B that the product doesn't require wastes capital. Specific limits and grades vary by jurisdiction, so validate against your own licence before building.
points: KPIs to run the system: >=98% right-first-time, <5% deviation rate, <=30d CAPA closure, <24h mock-recall
Qualify before you produce: V-model DQ to IQ to OQ to PQ, then 3 conformance batches for process validation
Cleaning is validated to a calculated MACO carryover limit with swab/rinse, TOC/HPLC, and micro acceptance criteria
Review cadence is fixed: per-batch, weekly, monthly, quarterly management review, annual PQR and self-inspection

figures.name: Review cadence

claims_needing_cite: Process validation conventionally requires three consecutive conforming batches
Cleaning validation acceptance is based on a calculated Maximum Allowable Carryover (MACO) limit
Equipment qualification follows the IQ/OQ/PQ progression within a validation V-model
